text: So hello everyone and welcome to this masterclass on laparoscopic bowel resection for endometriosis. So as you can see this is a patient with severe endometriosis who has been operated before. The preoperative evaluation demonstrated a para rectal nodule. Now this is a little atypical nodule. This is not the routine type of nodules which we see and these type of nodules cause something called as bowel on bowel syndrome which you will see here where the sigmoid is completely plastered over the anterior aspect of the rectum predominantly due to the fibrosis that has been introduced because of the nodule.

in this entire video we are going to demonstrate certain important steps of bowel adhesiolysis this is a conservative surgery she is a lady with primary infertility so the uterus is going to be conserved we are not going to be demonstrating the entire surgery here it is going to be demonstration of the bowel adhesiolysis along with bowel resection Now, as we have stated in our multiple videos before, the first and the most important step is identification of the ureter opening of the pararectal space and trying to see to it that the entire lesion and the nodule is centralized. Or let me put it the other way. The ureter is lateralized. So in this approach, as you can see, the bowel dissection the initial bowel dissection has been done on both the sides as you can see here and further steps of bowel adhesiolysis are going to be discussed in this entire video

The surgeon opens the pararectal spaces on both sides, creating a large pararectal window. This dissection through the pararectal and parasigmoidal fat is visible, ensuring the protection of the bowel lumen. The presence of thickened uterosacral ligaments and peritoneum is noted, with the surgeon navigating through a native tissue plane for safe and efficient dissection.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer to approach the anterior aspect of the rectum, exposing the anterior lateral aspect. The sigmoid colon appears coiled, with nodular growth causing impingement on the posterior side.

The surgeon uses a Bissinger bipolar instrument to gently grasp tissues and apply counter traction around the uterus. The laparoscopic instruments provide haptic feedback, allowing the surgeon to identify a nodule with a stone-like consistency. The surgeon attempts to cut through this consistency to expose the nodule for complete dissection.

The surgeon cuts through a stony hard nodule using a needle holder to provide cranial traction to the uterus. The nodule's consistency causes difficulty in traction, and the rectum appears coiled due to nodularity in the para rectal margins.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the anterior aspect of the rectum, assessing the remaining rectal length from the anorectal junction for bowel resection. The fibrosed nodule causes bowel dilatation above it, and the surgeon dissects the bowel below the nodule from the Denonvilliers fascia, ensuring approximately nine centimeters of bowel length remains below the nodule.

The surgeon uses suction to separate the nodule from its posterior vaginal attachment, exposing the pararectal fossa. The Bissinger bipolar is employed for gentle dissection, revealing a native tissue plane. The dissection continues to separate the anterolateral and anterior aspects of the rectum up to the perineal body, with similar dissection attempted on the opposite side.

The surgeon approaches the anterior aspect of the rectum through the anterolateral plane, making a bold incision through the anterior margin. This incision releases the bowel, visibly liberating it from the fibrous cluster induced by the nodularity in the pararectal region.

The surgeon has dissected the entire bowel through its length up to the perineal body on the anterior side, revealing the nodule that has caused puckering over the bowel region. The unhealthy bowel is visible, and the surgeon identifies the need for bowel resection due to the presence of a large area requiring removal, approximately seven to eleven centimeters in length.
The surgeon prepares the proximal and distal ends of the bowel for resection and anastomosis, ensuring that the anastomotic margins are free from interfering fat tissue to promote optimal healing.

The surgeon prepares the distal stump of the bowel for anastomosis, exposing the pararectal fat to a depth of approximately 0.5 centimeters to preserve vascularity. Using laparoscopic shearers, the surgeon performs circumferential dissection, revealing the lumen of the healthy rectum, which is crucial for ensuring a well-prepared anastomotic site.

The other question which people ask is that is there a need for a colorectal surgeon to be in the operation theater when this surgery is being performed? So the answer to that is no, because remember, these type of surgeries need expertise and expertise is not dependent on the field. You will be surprised to know quite a lot of bowel resections for endometriosis across the world are predominantly done by gynecologists. and not by colorectal surgeons. However, if there are country-specific regulations, then I would request people to follow the guidelines and regulations of their own country. When I speak about India, a person who is competent enough of doing this surgery must handle it. If you look at our center, we have probably the highest number of volume for doing bowel resection for advanced endometriosis in our country and quite a lot of videos quite a lot of presentations and of course we have our data with that so i think that is something which we bank upon should there be a complication another thing we always notify these things in our consent to our patients

The surgeon prepares the distal stump for bowel resection and inserts the linear Endo GIA stapler, ensuring the bowel is properly aligned within the stapler blades. The surgical field is checked to confirm no other structures, such as the ureter, are within the stapler's path. The port is widened to accommodate the stapler, which is then fired to transect the bowel.
The surgeon positions the bowel correctly before firing the linear stapler, ensuring a clean cut. The bowel size allows for a single firing of the stapler, which is sufficient for the resection.

The surgeon fires the linear endo GI stapler, creating a three-row linear cut through the distal stump. The stapler is then removed, and the remaining stump is refashioned using scissors to ensure no interference. The same preparation is performed on the proximal stump laparoscopically. A small suprapubic incision is made, and a bowel clamp is inserted through a 5mm port to facilitate the resection, with scissors used to cut the bowel.
The surgeon opens the lumen on the proximal end of the bowel, ensuring a clean cut through the tissue. The next step involves fixing the anvil inside the bowel for the anastomosis.

The surgeon makes a small suprapubic incision, approximately three centimeters, to insert the anvil for the circular stapler. A purse string suture is tied using polyglactin (vicryl) to secure the anvil, ensuring it is snug. The bowel is then repositioned with the anvil back inside the peritoneal cavity, maintaining a minimally invasive approach.

The surgeon passes the circular stapler through the anus, positioning it on the anterior aspect of the distal stump for an end-to-side anastomosis. The circular stapler pin enters the long distal stump, and the anvil is fitted through. The orange mark on the stapler indicates readiness to fire, ensuring the creation of intact donuts for histopathological examination.

The surgeon fits the anvil onto the opposite end of the circular stapler, verifying the alignment of all edges. The orange mark on the stapler becomes visible, indicating readiness for firing. The anastomotic site is checked for adequate vascularization before proceeding.

The surgeon prepares to fire the circular stapler, ensuring the anti-mycentric to anti-mycentric border of the colon is properly aligned to prevent twisting. The orange mark on the stapler is visible, indicating readiness for firing. The mucosal aspect is dissected, providing a clear view of the surgical site.

The circular stapler is fired and held in place for ten seconds before being gently released and removed through the anal canal. The uterus remains intact, indicating a conservative approach. The staple line is visible, confirming a successful end-to-side anastomosis, with the presence of both donuts from the anvil.
